metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises at least one full init entry point end-to-end (e.g., `run git rebar init` on an already-initialized repo and assert: no abort, `.ensure-applied` written, warning logged for any failed unit) to prove the cutover wiring is correct.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the wired init entry points (init_core / _init_via_symlink) end-to-end after the cutover. AC3 tests run_ensures() in isolation; the critical new code path — the actual init call chain calling run_ensures() instead of the hand-listed helpers — is never exercised by any stated criterion, so the cutover could be broken (wrong call site, argument mismatch, wrong lock order) and all ACs could still pass.
findings.location: AC bullets (all 6) and Scope / Wire section
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'End-to-end smoke: calling init_core (or _init_via_symlink) on an already-initialized tracker store completes without exception, writes a valid `.ensure-applied` JSON array, and emits no warning for a healthy store; verified by an integration test or a CLI invocation + `jq -e . .rebar/.ensure-applied`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end behavioral AC that calls `init_core` (or `_init_via_symlink`) on an already-inited store and asserts `.ensure-applied` is written containing the expected unit ids, confirming the init wiring (not just `run_ensures()` in isolation) is exercised.
findings.finding: The ACs test `run_ensures()` in isolation but specify no end-to-end behavioral test that exercises the init-entry-point wiring (i.e., calling `init_core` or `_init_via_symlink` and asserting that `.ensure-applied` is written or that ensures actually ran). This is a proxy-validation gap for the cutover: a test suite can go green (all run_ensures() unit/integration tests pass) while the wiring in the re-init arm, fresh-init arm, or _init_via_symlink is silently broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, AC4; 'Wire run_ensures() at ALL init entry points' in the What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Calling init_core on an existing store (re-init path) results in .ensure-applied being written with all 5 unit ids; calling _init_via_symlink likewise produces .ensure-applied — verified by an integration test that invokes the init entry point, not run_ensures() directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'six calls' count in the What/Scope section to 'eight calls at init.py:241-248, of which four (gitignore:242, gitattributes:245, gc-config:247, merge-ours-driver:248) are the migrated units to remove'.
findings.finding: The plan says 'six calls at init.py:241-248' but there are actually eight calls (lines 241-248 each hold exactly one call: _ensure_branch_user_config, _commit_gitignore, _exclude_scratch_in_tracker, _commit_precommit, _commit_gitattributes, _gen_local_files, _migrate_gc_config, _ensure_merge_ours_driver). The plan's count of 'six' is wrong; only four of them are the migrated units.
findings.location: What section: 'the six calls at init.py:241-248'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the six calls at init.py:241-248' with 'the eight calls at init.py:241-248, of which four (lines 242, 245, 247, 248) are the migrated units to replace with run_ensures()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC2: e.g., 'a unit test injects a raising callable into the registry, calls run_ensures(), asserts the outcome list contains a `failed` EnsureOutcome for that id, and asserts `.ensure-applied` does NOT contain that id.'
findings.finding: AC2 states that 'a raising unit is caught (skip-and-continue → failed) and excluded from `.ensure-applied`' as a behavioral spec but provides no explicit proving command or test description. The fault-injection path (a unit that raises) has no stated mechanism for verification.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'a raising unit is caught (skip-and-continue → failed) and excluded from `.ensure-applied`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_ensure_env_id` has NO call site in the fresh-init arm (lines 241-248); the 'env-id' ensure unit covers the re-init arm (line 209) and both symlink-init sites (lines 564, 582) — the plan should specify how the ensure unit relates to the `_gen_local_files` env-id minting path on fresh init (whether `_gen_local_files` keeps its own minting and the ensure unit is re-init/symlink-only, or `_gen_local_files` is refactored to call through the unit).
findings.finding: The plan claims `_ensure_env_id` must be removed from 'the fresh-init arm (the six calls at init.py:241-248)', but reading init.py:241-248 shows `_ensure_env_id` has NO call site there. Fresh-init env-id creation is handled by `_gen_local_files` (line 246). The `_ensure_env_id` call sites are only at line 209 (re-init arm), 564, and 582 (both `_init_via_symlink`). Treating `_ensure_env_id` as a fresh-init call to remove is a false premise — the 'env-id' ensure unit wiring for the fresh-init arm needs to replace `_gen_local_files`'s env-id logic, not a `_ensure_env_id` call that doesn't exist there.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire run_ensures() at ALL init entry points and remove the hand-listed calls: … the fresh-init arm (the six calls at init.py:241-248 — of which gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge are the migrated units)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure mode for write-lock acquisition in `run_ensures()`: either wrap the lock call in a try/except (log + proceed without running ensures, or re-raise with a clear message) or document a timeout, so the 'never aborts on an ensure failure' guarantee extends to lock-level failures.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `run_ensures()` executes under the store write lock but does not specify the failure mode when lock acquisition itself blocks indefinitely or raises. Because only per-unit exceptions are caught and the 'never aborts' guarantee is stated only for unit-level failures, a lock-level failure (timeout, deadlock, or an exception from `write_lock`) would propagate uncaught to the init entry point and abort initialization — violating the stated resilience goal.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock … catches a raising unit (skip-and-continue → failed)'; AC line 2: 'init logs failures and never aborts'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `with write_lock(tracker):` block in a try/except; on failure log a warning (e.g. 'ensure registry skipped: could not acquire store lock') and return an empty outcome list, preserving the no-abort contract. Document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for `run_ensures()` when `write_lock` raises `LockTimeout` (e.g., propagate as-is, or return a synthetic `[EnsureOutcome(id='<lock>', status='failed', detail='lock timeout')]` list) so the no-abort guarantee's scope is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback for a `write_lock` acquisition failure inside `run_ensures()`. The 'never aborts on an ensure failure' guarantee covers unit-level exceptions but not a `LockTimeout` raised by `lock.acquire` before any unit runs. An implementor has no spec-grounded decision: propagate the exception (violating the no-abort contract at the call site), swallow it silently (returns an empty outcome list with no indication of failure), or degrade gracefully (return a synthetic failed outcome). This is an unspecified fallback for an incomplete sub-step.
findings.location: What section — 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock'; AC2 — 'init logs failures and never aborts'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the fresh-init arm description: `_ensure_env_id` does NOT appear at init.py:241-248; its three call sites are line 209 (re-init arm) and lines 564/582 (`_init_via_symlink`) — update the plan text to enumerate these accurately so AC4's 'no other callers remain' check is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan's wiring instruction for the fresh-init arm incorrectly states that `_ensure_env_id` appears among 'the six calls at init.py:241-248'. Code inspection shows `_ensure_env_id` has NO call site at lines 241-248 — those lines contain `_ensure_branch_user_config`, `_commit_gitignore`, `_exclude_scratch_in_tracker`, `_commit_precommit`, `_commit_gitattributes`, `_gen_local_files`, `_migrate_gc_config`, and `_ensure_merge_ours_driver`. `_ensure_env_id` appears only at line 209 (re-init arm) and lines 564/582 (`_init_via_symlink`). This factual error in the spec creates a pink-elephant antipattern: an implementor searching for a fresh-init `_ensure_env_id` call to remove will not find it, may miscount the total caller set, and may misread the instruction 'remove the hand-listed calls from the fresh-init arm' as requiring action where none is needed.
findings.location: What section — 'the fresh-init arm (the six calls at init.py:241-248 — of which gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge are the migrated units; `_ensure_branch_user_config` is bootstrap and stays)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the six calls at init.py:241-248 — of which gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge are the migrated units' with 'the five calls at init.py:241-248 — gitignore(242), gitattributes(245), gc-config(247), merge-ours(248) are the migrated units; _ensure_env_id does NOT appear here (its three sites are line 209 and lines 564/582 in _init_via_symlink)'.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `env-id` ensure unit replaces the `.env-id` creation inside `_gen_local_files` (requiring a split of that function) or whether `_gen_local_files` retains `.env-id` creation and the ensure unit only covers re-init/symlink paths; update the migration spec and 'no double-execution' AC accordingly.
findings.finding: The `env-id` ensure unit's fresh-init coverage is ambiguous: in the fresh-init arm (lines 241-248), `.env-id` is created inside `_gen_local_files` (line 246/481-484), NOT via `_ensure_env_id`. The plan migrates `env-id` as an ensure unit and lists `_ensure_env_id`'s three call sites (209, 564, 582) — but `_gen_local_files` also creates `.env-id` and the plan says `_gen_local_files` stays as a bootstrap step. After migration, both the new `env-id` ensure unit AND the retained `_gen_local_files` will attempt to create `.env-id` on fresh init.
findings.location: What section: 'Migrate exactly the 5 re-init idempotent units'; AC line: 'no double-execution; no other callers of the 5 helpers remain'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `run_ensures()` in the fresh-init arm is called inside or outside `_acquire_init_lock`; state explicitly that the fresh-init nesting (init-mkdir-lock → store-write-lock) is safe because no code path acquires them in the inverse order.
findings.finding: The plan states 'init_core does NOT currently hold [the write lock]; this is a new dependency' but does not acknowledge that the fresh-init arm of init_core already holds a different lock (_acquire_init_lock / `.git/ticket-init.lock`) BEFORE run_ensures() would be called. Placing run_ensures() inside the fresh-init try-block (the described wiring location) means that code path will hold init-mkdir-lock → store-write-lock as a nested pair. The plan never calls out that this nesting is intentional and safe, leaving the implementer without guidance on whether run_ensures() should be called inside or outside the init-mkdir-lock, and whether any other path could produce the inverse ordering and create a deadlock.
findings.location: What section — 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock'; init.py:233-255 (fresh-init arm holding _acquire_init_lock)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the 'What' section: 'In the fresh-init arm, run_ensures() is called inside the existing init-mkdir-lock (`_acquire_init_lock`) try-block, after the bootstrap steps — this nesting (init-mkdir-lock → store-write-lock) is safe because no other code path acquires the store write-lock first and then waits for the init-mkdir-lock.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or fold into AC3/AC4) that verifies the stale `_migrate_gc_config` docstring reference in `sync.py:17` is updated.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes updating the stale docstring reference in `sync.py:17`, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this deliverable — it is an orphaned described work item.
findings.location: What section (last sentence) / Acceptance Criteria (all 6 ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: '…and the stale `_migrate_gc_config` docstring reference in `sync.py:17` is updated to reflect the new ensure-registry name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc section capturing: the ensure-registry pattern rationale (desired-state vs. hand-listed calls), the `EnsureOutcome` contract, the `.ensure-applied` file format, and the new write-lock dependency at init time.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern (idempotent ensure-registry, desired-state convergence at every entry point, new `.ensure-applied` file format/contract, new `EnsureOutcome` dataclass API) but plans no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture the decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNNN-ensure-registry.md` (or a `## Ensure Registry` section in CLAUDE.md) covering: (1) why desired-state over hand-listed calls, (2) the `EnsureOutcome` contract and stable-id invariant, (3) the `.ensure-applied` format (JSON array, full-rewrite semantics), (4) why the write lock is acquired and that init_core previously did not hold it, (5) what belongs in the registry vs. bootstrap-only steps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add init.py:8 (module docstring reference to `_migrate_gc_config`) to the list of docstring updates alongside sync.py:17.
findings.finding: The init.py module-level docstring (line 8) also references `_migrate_gc_config` by name ('see `_migrate_gc_config`'), but the plan only lists `sync.py:17` as the stale docstring to update. This is an unenumerated consumer of the rename.
findings.location: What section: 'Update the stale docstring ref to `_migrate_gc_config` in `sync.py:17`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change to: 'Update the stale docstring refs to `_migrate_gc_config` in `sync.py:17` and `init.py:8`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan whether _init_via_symlink (and any other wired entry point) currently holds the write lock, and whether write_lock is re-entrant, so the executor knows if nested acquisition is safe.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `init_core` does NOT currently hold the write lock, but says nothing about whether `_init_via_symlink` (or any other wired entry point) also does not hold it, and does not state whether the lock is re-entrant. If any entry point already holds the write lock when run_ensures() tries to acquire it, a deadlock results.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock (_store/lock.py write_lock — init_core does NOT currently hold it; this is a new dependency)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Neither _init_via_symlink nor any other wired entry point currently holds the write lock at the point run_ensures() is inserted; write_lock is [re-entrant / non-re-entrant] so [nesting is safe / is not a concern].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `write_lock` (_store/lock.py) is reentrant; if non-reentrant, confirm no existing caller holds it when invoking any init entry point, and add this as a pre-condition or guard in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new write-lock acquisition inside `run_ensures()` called from init_core, but does not address whether existing callers of init_core (or of the symlink-init path) already hold the write lock, which would cause a deadlock if the lock is non-reentrant.
findings.location: What section — 'runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock (_store/lock.py write_lock — init_core does NOT currently hold it; this is a new dependency)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and document in the plan) that `_init_via_symlink` does NOT currently hold the store write lock before adding the `run_ensures()` call there, so the new write-lock dependency does not introduce re-entrancy or deadlock on that path.
findings.finding: The plan notes that 'init_core does NOT currently hold [the write lock]; this is a new dependency' but does not confirm the same for the _init_via_symlink path — if symlink-init already acquires the write lock (or a re-entrant path does), wrapping run_ensures() in write_lock there could deadlock or double-acquire.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock' + '_init_via_symlink (which also calls _ensure_env_id at 564, 582)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note alongside the _init_via_symlink wiring point: 'lock state at call site: write lock not held (confirmed)' — mirroring the existing note for init_core — so the reviewer and implementer can verify this assumption without reading the full call graph.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `.ensure-applied` is written via `_store/fsutil.atomic_write` (not a bespoke inline rename) to avoid duplicating the existing crash-atomic write utility.
findings.finding: The plan specifies writing `.ensure-applied` 'atomically' but does not specify using the existing `_store/fsutil.atomic_write` utility, which already provides crash-atomic temp-in-same-dir + `os.replace` semantics and has 14+ call-sites across the codebase. An implementer may write a bespoke atomic-rename instead, duplicating functionality that already exists.
findings.location: What section: 'atomically rewrites `.ensure-applied`'; AC line 5: '`.ensure-applied` written atomically'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'atomically rewrites `.ensure-applied`' with 'atomically rewrites `.ensure-applied` via `_store/fsutil.atomic_write`'. Add a corresponding note in AC 5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the post-rollback assertion to explicitly cover git-config state: confirm old code tolerates `gc.autoDetach=true` and absent `gc.auto`, and `merge.ours.driver=true`, or document a manual `git config` rollback step for these entries.
findings.finding: The plan's post-rollback assertion covers only git-ignored `.ensure-applied` and committed branch files (`.gitattributes`, `.gitignore`), but says nothing about the STATE of git-config after rollback. The `gc-config` unit writes `gc.autoDetach=true` and unsets `gc.auto`; the `merge-ours-driver` unit writes `merge.ours.driver=true` into local git config. Rolling back the Python code does NOT undo these `git config` mutations — they persist in the tracker's `.git/config`. The plan does not assert whether old code tolerates these configs being present (likely harmless, but unasserted), and does not provide a compensating-forward or manual rollback step for them.
findings.location: What section, 'Post-rollback' paragraph; AC5 (.ensure-applied rollback); Scope 'Out' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Post-rollback' paragraph: 'Git-config mutations (`gc.autoDetach=true`, `merge.ours.driver=true`, unset `gc.auto`) persist in the tracker's `.git/config` after code rollback. Old code that previously wrote `gc.auto=0` will not re-apply it (a no-op for correctness since union recovery keeps all refs reachable). Manual rollback: `git config --unset gc.autoDetach; git config gc.auto 0; git config --remove-section merge.ours` if strict old-code compatibility is required.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
